stages.name: Ориентир
stages.text: A few steps from Wall Street, on Bowling Green, a bronze bull lowers its head and digs in as if the next second will be a charge. Charging Bull is both a landmark and shorthand: in one silhouette, it compresses the market's favourite story — risk, momentum, stubborn confidence. People don't come here for art history; they come for a ritual, a photo, a touch for luck, as if optimism could be made physical.

stages.name: Профиль и размеры
stages.text: Side-on, the proportions read true. 3.4 metres at the shoulder. 4.9 metres nose to tail. Heavier than a Manhattan delivery truck, low enough that tourists climb on its back for the photo. Di Modica wanted the silhouette of an animal mid-charge — not a monument — and the choice of pose forced everything else: a heavy front quarter, a planted hind, the centre of gravity dropped forward.

stages.name: Разлёт · секции
stages.text: You can't cast a thing this size as a single piece — no foundry's kiln takes three tonnes of molten metal in one pour. So Arturo Di Modica cast it in sections at Bedi-Makky Art Foundry in Greenpoint, Brooklyn, then welded and hand-finished the seams until the joints read as skin. Pulled apart, the sections show how the bull was actually built.

stages.name: Морда и рога
stages.text: Head-on, the bull is one of the city’s most photographed corners. There’s almost always a small crowd here: palms on the muzzle, business cards pressed to the horns, a quick rub for luck before the next photo. The nose and the tips of the horns are noticeably lighter than the rest of the body — patina worn away by years of touch, a place of power for people who hope a little of the animal’s momentum rubs off in return.

stages.name: Вид сзади
stages.text: From behind, the bull keeps the same charging momentum — tail and rump and the slope of a back arched for the strike. The most-touched spot of all is here: visitors duck beneath the belly, sit for a photograph, and steady themselves by leaning on the joints of the hind legs — which is why those joints are polished smooth too. Power, virility, force — the same animal symbol, told from a different angle.
